message:
fix(db,cli,server,tools): case-insensitive agent resolution everywhere, resilient post-commit probes, TUI thread detail stability, and unread-only inbox filtering

Case-insensitive agent name resolution across all query surfaces
--------------------------------------------------------------
Agent names were resolved case-sensitively in several query paths
(mail_explorer, search_planner, global inbox, global unread count,
system agent insert, contact policy update, TUI explorer filters)
despite the schema's NOCASE unique index. This caused silent misses
when callers used a different casing than the canonical name stored in
the database — a common situation because MCP tool invocations do not
guarantee consistent casing.

All agent name comparisons in SQL now use `COLLATE NOCASE` explicitly,
and the CLI's centralized `resolve_agent()` fetches LIMIT 2 rows to
detect legacy case-duplicate states left behind before the NOCASE
migration. If two rows match, the function returns an unambiguous error
directing the operator to run `am migrate`, rather than silently
picking one. This is important because pre-migration databases may
have both "BlueLake" and "bluelake" as separate agent rows, and
guessing wrong could route messages to the wrong agent.

The CLI's `lib.rs` had 6+ copy-pasted inline agent-lookup blocks
(file reservations, acks, contacts) that each did their own
case-sensitive SELECT. These are all replaced with calls to the shared
`context::resolve_agent()`, which is both DRYer and guarantees
case-insensitive + ambiguity-safe behavior everywhere.

Robot command agent resolution made fallible and explicit
--------------------------------------------------------
`robot.rs`'s `resolve_agent_id()` previously returned `Option` and
swallowed all errors, meaning an ambiguous-agent error from the new
LIMIT 2 detection would silently become None. It now returns
`Result<Option<...>>`, and a new `soften_implicit_missing_agent_error()`
helper distinguishes between:
  - explicit --agent flag with a missing name → hard error (user typo)
  - implicit env-var agent with a missing name → soft None (tolerable)
  - ambiguous legacy duplicates → always hard error (never guess)

Resilient post-commit durability probes
---------------------------------------
The durability probes after `register_agent`, `create_agent`, and
`create_message_with_recipients` previously treated ALL probe failures
as fatal, blocking the caller even though the underlying commit had
already succeeded. This caused spurious 500s during transient SQLite
contention (e.g., concurrent WAL checkpointing or lock contention
from another process).

Now, only "hard" errors (Internal, IntegrityCorruption, Schema) are
treated as fatal. Transient probe failures (Sqlite contention,
Cancelled, Panicked) are logged as advisory warnings and the
already-committed result is returned. This avoids the antipattern
where a successful write is hidden from the caller because a
read-after-write probe happened to fail.

For message creation specifically, hard post-commit failures now
trigger `cleanup_committed_message_after_consistency_failure()`, which
deletes the orphaned message + recipients and rebuilds `inbox_stats`
for affected agents in a single transaction, so the system does not
accumulate phantom messages that passed commit but failed integrity
verification.

The durability probe connection itself now uses
`open_sqlite_file_with_recovery()` and applies `PRAGMA_CONN_SETTINGS_SQL`,
matching what pool connections do, which prevents the probe from
failing simply because it opened a bare connection without WAL mode
or busy_timeout configured.

Fetch-inbox unread-only variant (fetch_inbox_unread)
----------------------------------------------------
The `views_urgent_unread` resource was calling `fetch_inbox` (which
returns all messages including read ones) and then filtering in Rust.
This was both wasteful and incorrect — it included already-read urgent
messages in the "urgent unread" view. A new `fetch_inbox_unread()`
variant adds `AND r.read_ts IS NULL` at the SQL level, and the
`InboxRow` struct now includes `read_ts` so callers can distinguish
read/unread without extra queries.

TUI thread detail panel stability
---------------------------------
The thread explorer's detail panel had two UX problems:
1. When the thread list refreshed (e.g., new message arrived, re-sort),
   the cursor could silently jump to a different thread because the
   position was clamped by index rather than preserved by thread_id.
2. When the selected thread received a new message, the detail panel
   reset to page zero, losing the operator's scroll position, expanded
   messages, and collapsed tree nodes — forcing them to manually
   re-navigate.

Now `refresh_thread_list()` saves the selected thread_id before
refresh and restores the cursor to the same thread afterward.
`refresh_detail_if_needed()` detects same-thread updates by comparing
message_count and last_timestamp, and when the thread is the same,
preserves all UI state (cursor, scroll, expanded/collapsed sets,
tree focus, load-older flag) while loading additional messages into
the existing window.

A new `clear_detail_state()` helper centralizes the reset-to-empty
logic that was previously duplicated in multiple branches.

TUI message browser live-event fallback and body search
-------------------------------------------------------
When the DB connection is unavailable, the message browser now falls
back to searching the live event ring instead of silently returning
nothing. This is surfaced via a new `SearchMethod::Live` variant so
the status bar shows "live-events" to indicate degraded mode.

The live-event text search also now includes `body_md` in the
searchable haystack, so body content is findable even in degraded mode.

Server startup refactoring
--------------------------
`run_http()`'s startup sequence is extracted into
`prepare_http_runtime_startup()` and
`note_startup_integrity_probe_if_enabled()` for clarity and reuse.
The TUI readiness warmup functions are relocated next to their callers.
Test ordering is adjusted to match the new function ordering.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
